serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of beef with black bean sauce is approximately 200 g, representing one individual portion as a shared dish alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Beef with black bean sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Beef with black bean sauce' (CR ID: F06040010). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed beef and fermented legume dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef with black bean sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on: (1) very low carbohydrate content of 3.99 g/100g, (2) fermented black beans having a documented low GI (~20–30) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for legumes, (3) high protein content (21.39 g/100g) providing strong glycaemic buffering. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 3.99 × 2) / 100 = 2.79, rounded to 2.8. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 784 mg/100g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100g hypertension threshold, driven by fermented black beans, soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g, request low-sodium soy sauce and reduced oyster sauce, and rinse fermented black beans before cooking; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 1,568 mg sodium, representing over 65% of the 2,400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.99 g/100g), low sugar (2.21 g/100g), and high protein (21.39 g/100g) provide strong glycaemic buffering with an estimated GI of 35
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef with black bean sauce is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when served without large portions of white rice; the estimated glycemic load per 200 g serving is approximately 2.8 (very low), and the high protein content supports postprandial glucose stability, though total meal carbohydrate from accompanying dishes should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (784 mg/100g) raises blood pressure risk; saturated fat from beef contributes to LDL elevation; however, iron (2.34 mg/100g) and protein are beneficial
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to one small serving (100 g) per meal, request preparation with reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium load, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to offset the saturated fat contribution from beef.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory cytokines; sodium at 784 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; bell peppers are nightshade vegetables that may trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request omission of bell peppers, substitute beef with tofu or tempeh to eliminate arachidonic acid load, and use low-sodium soy sauce to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef with black bean sauce (豉汁炒牛肉, Chǐ zhī chǎo niúròu) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean stir-fried dish consisting of thinly sliced beef wok-tossed with fermented black beans (douchi), garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and bell peppers, finished with a cornstarch-thickened glaze and a drizzle of sesame oil. The dish delivers a bold, savoury, and slightly umami-rich flavour profile with tender beef and a glossy, deeply seasoned sauce. It is commonly served as a shared main dish alongside steamed white rice in Chinese-Singaporean hawker centres and restaurants. Nutritionally, it is high in protein (21.39 g/100g) and moderate in fat (11.2 g/100g), with very low carbohydrate (3.99 g/100g) and low sugar (2.21 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. However, sodium content is very high at 784 mg/100g, driven by fermented black beans, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is 35 (low), and the glycemic load per 200 g serving is approximately 2.8, making it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion-controlled and paired with low-GI accompaniments. Iron content is notable at 2.34 mg/100g, contributing to daily iron requirements. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean hawker contexts when prepared with halal-certified beef and sauces.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06040010), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All values are exact matches with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. GI is an analogous estimate documented with full reasoning.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific prepared dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F06040010 as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef stir-fried dish (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB does not report this breakdown for this item. The input record listed 'plant_based' as a dietary tag, which is incorrect for a beef dish and has been removed. The 'halal' tag is retained as appropriate for halal-certified beef preparations. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. FoodEx2 match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
